novel_features.name: Commute-blocked check
novel_features.description: Plan a journey A→B and immediately see open road hazards near the journey's endpoints plus active alerts on the lines it uses — one verdict on whether your route is clear.
novel_features.rationale: Joins Trip Planner trip legs against open road hazards (by proximity to the journey's endpoints) and Trip Planner service-status messages (by line) — no single API call or existing tool fuses transit, roads, and line alerts.
novel_features.why_it_matters: When a user asks 'is my route clear right now', this is the one call that checks the journey, nearby road hazards, and the lines it uses together.

novel_features.name: Cheapest fuel near a place
novel_features.description: Find the cheapest station for a fuel type within N km of a named stop/POI or a planned trip's destination, ranked by price.
novel_features.rationale: Resolves a place name to coordinates via the local stops store, then ranks the local fuel_prices table by distance — works offline; the only fuel library has no 'cheapest near me' and no place-name input.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Answers 'where should I fill up near X' without the user knowing any coordinates.

novel_features.name: Where's my bus on a route
novel_features.description: Show live GTFS-Realtime vehicle positions on a named route or line, ranked by distance from a chosen stop.
novel_features.rationale: Decodes the GTFS-RT vehicle-positions protobuf, filters to one route_id, and ranks vehicles by distance to a stop from the local stops table — the only existing GTFS-RT tool is a DB pipeline with no live query.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Direct answer to 'where is the 333 right now and how far from my stop' — the top user workflow.

novel_features.name: Place snapshot
novel_features.description: Everything about one stop in a single call: next departures, line alerts, the nearest traffic cameras, and the cheapest nearby fuel.
novel_features.rationale: Joins a live departure board with line alerts, the nearest traffic cameras, and the cheapest nearby fuel — the canonical 'tell me everything about this stop' query that no single API returns.
novel_features.why_it_matters: When an agent is asked about a specific stop, this is the one call that returns the full picture.

novel_features.name: Cameras near a location
novel_features.description: List traffic cameras within N km of a stop or a lat/lng, with their JPG hrefs and optional download.
novel_features.rationale: Local cameras table plus distance math — the live endpoint is one un-paged GeoJSON blob with no proximity query.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Lets a user eyeball the road near a place without scrolling hundreds of cameras.

novel_features.name: Network disruptions, all modes
novel_features.description: Today's disruptions across every transport mode — GTFS-RT service alerts unioned with Trip Planner service-status messages, deduped and severity-sorted.
novel_features.rationale: Reads the local alerts table, which normalizes two different alert sources into one deduped, ranked list — neither source endpoint gives the union.
novel_features.why_it_matters: One call answers 'what's broken on the network right now' instead of polling per-mode feeds.

novel_features.name: Fuel price drift since last sync
novel_features.description: Show the price change per station for a fuel type since the last snapshot (recorded by `refresh` / `fuel prices`).
novel_features.rationale: Diffs the two most recent fuel-price snapshots in local SQLite — there is no API endpoint for 'what changed since I last looked'.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Surfaces the actionable signal (prices moved at specific stations) that the raw FuelCheck price endpoints can't.

narrative.headline: Every NSW transport, traffic, and fuel feed in one CLI, plus cross-source commands no single API offers.
narrative.value_prop: Wraps five scattered NSW open-data APIs — GTFS-Realtime transit feeds, the Trip Planner, Live Traffic hazards, Live Traffic cameras, and FuelCheck — behind one binary with `--json`, typed exit codes, and a local SQLite store backing `search`/`sql` and the fuel-price history. On top of matching every Home Assistant integration and the Raycast extension, it adds commands those tools can't: `commute` (is my route blocked — checking the journey, nearby road hazards, and line alerts together), `fuel-stop` (cheapest fuel near a named place), `whereis` (live vehicle positions on a route, ranked by distance from your stop), and `station` (a stop's departures, alerts, nearby cameras, and cheapest nearby fuel in one call). First tool — and first agent surface — to treat Sydney location intelligence as one thing.
narrative.auth_narrative: Most commands use a free Transport Open Data Hub API key in the `NSW_OPENDATA_API_KEY` env var (register an app at opendata.transport.nsw.gov.au and subscribe to the GTFS-Realtime, Trip Planner, and Live Traffic products). The `fuel ...` commands additionally need free FuelCheck credentials (`NSW_FUELCHECK_API_KEY` + `NSW_FUELCHECK_API_SECRET` from api.nsw.gov.au) — the CLI does the OAuth2 handshake and caches the token; without them the fuel commands fail with an actionable message and everything else still works.

quickstart.comment: confirm NSW_OPENDATA_API_KEY is set and the Open Data Hub is reachable
find a stop ID to use — Central Station is 10101100
next departures from Central, with realtime estimates
live positions of every Sydney Trains service right now
populate the local store so `search`/`sql` work offline (the cross-source commands fetch live)
cheapest E10 within 5 km of Central

troubleshoots.symptom: HTTP 503 "Your API quota or rate limit has been exceeded"
troubleshoots.fix: You are polling faster than the feeds refresh. Back off to one request per ~15 seconds; the realtime feeds only update every 10–15s anyway.

troubleshoots.symptom: HTTP 500 "Policy Falsified" on a realtime or live-traffic call
troubleshoots.fix: The Authorization header is malformed or your app isn't subscribed to that API product. Check `NSW_OPENDATA_API_KEY` has no quotes/whitespace and subscribe to the product in the Open Data Hub portal.
Use the default search (type any). The Aug-2025 Trip Planner upgrade broke name searches with type_sf=stop — this CLI already defaults to `any`.
Set `NSW_FUELCHECK_API_KEY` and `NSW_FUELCHECK_API_SECRET` from your api.nsw.gov.au FuelCheck app. They're separate from the Open Data Hub key.
Run `nsw-transport-pp-cli refresh` (or `fuel prices`) at least twice — each records a price snapshot. The other cross-source commands fetch live and need no sync.

narrative.when_to_use: Reach for this CLI for any Sydney/NSW location question that touches public transport, road traffic, traffic cameras, or fuel prices — next departures, journey planning, where a bus is, whether a route is blocked, what's disrupted on the network, or the cheapest fuel near somewhere. It's the right choice over a generic HTTP client because it decodes the GTFS-Realtime protobuf, does the FuelCheck OAuth handshake, normalizes GeoJSON, and decodes the GTFS-Realtime protobuf, does the FuelCheck OAuth handshake, normalizes GeoJSON, and joins the sources at query time — so a question like 'cheapest E10 near Town Hall' or 'is the train to the CBD OK right now' is one call, not five.

recipes.title: Is my morning commute clear?
recipes.explanation: Plans the journey and reports open hazards on the roads it uses plus active alerts on the lines it uses; the dotted --select paths narrow the nested line_alerts and nearby_hazards arrays to just titles and headlines.

recipes.title: Where is the 333 bus?
recipes.explanation: Decodes the live bus vehicle-positions feed, filters to route 333, and ranks vehicles by distance from stop 203311.

recipes.title: Cheapest diesel near where I'm driving to
recipes.explanation: Resolves the stop name to coordinates and ranks nearby stations by diesel price using the local store.

recipes.title: What's disrupted on the trains today (compact, agent-friendly)
recipes.explanation: Unions the GTFS-RT alerts and Trip Planner service-status messages for trains, deduped, and narrows each record to just the source, headline, severity, and affected lines.

recipes.title: Save a traffic camera image near a stop
recipes.explanation: Finds the nearest camera to the stop and downloads its current JPEG (cameras refresh roughly every 15 seconds).

trigger_phrases: plan a trip from Marrickville to the city
